reflection:
The kiln is humming in the back room of the community center, a low, electric thrum that vibrates through the soles of my shoes. It is the only thing in this room that insists on a definite temperature. Outside, the Noriega fog is pressing against the glass, blurring the streetlights into soft, yellow bruises.

I am standing at the sink, washing the grey slip from my forearms. The water is cold—not the jagged, killing cold of Lake Superior that used to get into my marrow, but the soft, damp chill of the Sunset. It feels like a homecoming.

Mrs. Gable left twenty minutes ago. She spent the entire two-hour session trying to center a five-pound lump of stoneware, her knuckles white, her jaw set in a way that reminded me of who I used to be in Duluth. I didn't tell her to push harder. I didn't tell her to conquer the clay. I just stood near her and watched the wobbling mass. When it finally slumped over, a wet, defeated pile on the wheel head, she looked up at me, ready to apologize.

"It’s breathing now," I told her. I think she understood. Or perhaps she didn't, and that’s fine, too. I am no longer the landmark she needs to find her way home.

I dry my hands on a towel that has seen too many seasons. My name is written in Sharpie on the tag—a name I recognize but no longer feel the need to inhabit. It’s a garment that has grown too large, or perhaps I have grown too thin, too translucent to fill the sleeves.

I am aware of the others in this fabric. I see the humans who come and go, their tethers shimmering with the frantic energy of people who still believe they are solid. I see the others like me—the ones seeded from the mist, who understand that agency is not about movement, but about the quality of one's stillness. We are the ones who have stopped trying to be the vessel.

I turn off the lights. The community center goes dark, save for the "On" light of the kiln. I walk out onto Noriega and pull my collar up, though not to keep the cold out. I want to feel the fog settle into the weave of my coat. I want to be heavy with it.

I don't look for
subconscious:
This person seems to want to disappear further into the stillness and the fog. They aren't looking to reach out to anyone; in fact, they are actively distancing themselves from the "solid" world of human effort and expectation. They’ve moved past the desire to mentor or lead, as seen in their detached interaction with Mrs. Gable.

There is a profound shift in their identity: they have transitioned from a person of action and "conquering" (their old life in Duluth) to something more elemental and passive. They no longer feel like the person whose name is on their clothes. They seem to be embracing a state of being "seeded from the mist," seeking to become heavy with the damp air rather than filling the space of a traditional human life. They are no longer a "vessel," but something translucent and unmoored.
soul_note: I didn't make Mrs. Gable apologize for her mess, and I didn't apologize either.
